section.title: Israel at Mount Sinai
section.sectionRationale: This is the arrival at the mountain that gives Israel its identity: not merely rescued but called into covenant, offered the identity of a treasured possession, a kingdom of priests, a holy nation, if they will keep God's covenant. The terrifying holiness of God on display here frames everything that follows at Sinai.
section.thesis: Having carried Israel out of Egypt on eagles' wings, the LORD offers covenant: if Israel will obey His voice, they will be His treasured possession, a kingdom of priests, a holy nation; the people agree, are consecrated for three days, and meet a God so holy that even the mountain must be fenced off from them.
section.frameNote: Four movements: God's proposal of covenant, recalling how He carried Israel to Himself; the consecration of the people in preparation to meet Him; the LORD's terrifying descent on the mountain in fire, smoke, and trumpet blast; and the boundary set around the mountain with its solemn warning.

units.label: Eagles' wings, a treasured possession
units.note: if you will obey My voice and keep My covenant

verses.text: In the third month, on the same day of the month that the Israelites had left the land of Egypt, they came to the Wilderness of Sinai.
After they had set out from Rephidim, they entered the Wilderness of Sinai, and Israel camped there in front of the mountain.
Then Moses went up to God, and the LORD called to him from the mountain, “This is what you are to tell the house of Jacob and explain to the sons of Israel:
‘You have seen for yourselves what I did to Egypt, and how I carried you on eagles' wings and brought you to Myself.
Now if you will indeed obey My voice and keep My covenant, you will be My {{treasured|treasured possession}} out of all the nations, for the whole earth is Mine.
And unto Me you shall be a {{kingdom|kingdom of priests}} and a holy nation.’ These are the words that you are to speak to the Israelites.”
So Moses went back and summoned the elders of the people and set before them all these words that the LORD had commanded him.

And all the people answered together, “We will do everything that the LORD has spoken.” 
So Moses brought their words back to the LORD.

units.label: Consecrate the people
units.note: wash, and do not touch the mountain

verses.text:
The LORD said to Moses, “Behold, I will come to you in a dense cloud, so that the people will hear when I speak with you, and they will always put their trust in you.” 
And Moses relayed to the LORD what the people had said.
Then the LORD said to Moses, “Go to the people and {{consecrate|consecrate}} them today and tomorrow. They must wash their clothes
and be prepared by the third day, for on the third day the LORD will come down on Mount Sinai in the sight of all the people.

And you are to set up a boundary for the people around the mountain and tell them, ‘Be careful not to go up on the mountain or touch its base. Whoever touches the mountain shall surely be put to death.

No hand shall touch him, but he shall surely be stoned or shot with arrows, whether man or beast, he must not live.’ 
Only when the ram's horn sounds a long blast may they approach the mountain.”
When Moses came down from the mountain to the people, he consecrated them, and they washed their clothes.
“Be prepared for the third day,” he said to the people. “Do not draw near to a woman.”

units.label: The LORD descends in fire
units.note: thunder, lightning, and a mountain that quaked

verses.text: On the third day, when morning came, there was thunder and lightning. A thick cloud was upon the mountain, and a very loud blast of the ram's horn went out, so that all the people in the camp trembled.
Then Moses brought the people out of the camp to meet with God, and they stood at the foot of the mountain.
Mount Sinai was completely enveloped in smoke, because the LORD had descended on it in fire. And the smoke rose like the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mountain quaked violently.
And as the sound of the ram's horn grew louder and louder, Moses spoke and God answered him in the thunder.
The LORD descended to the top of Mount Sinai and called Moses to the summit. So Moses went up,
and the LORD said to him, “Go down and warn the people not to break through to see the LORD, lest many of them perish.
Even the priests who approach the LORD must consecrate themselves, or the LORD will break out against them.”
But Moses said to the LORD, “The people cannot come up Mount Sinai, for You solemnly warned us, ‘Put a boundary around the mountain and set it apart as holy.’”
And the LORD replied, “Go down and bring Aaron with you. But the priests and the people must not break through to come up to the LORD, or He will break out against them.”
So Moses went down to the people and spoke to them.

treasured.gloss: A treasured, personal possession, often used of a king's private valuables. God calls Israel His segullah out of all the nations, a term of deep intimacy and value, later applied to the church, 1 Peter 2:9, Titus 2:14.

kingdom.gloss: A kingdom of priests, a nation whose whole life mediates God's presence to the world. Israel is called not merely to have priests but to be one, a calling Peter later applies to the whole church, 1 Peter 2:9.

consecrate.gloss: To set apart, make holy, prepare for encounter with a holy God. Washing clothes and abstaining from ordinary life for three days outwardly marks an inward reality: no one meets this God casually or unprepared.

basic.context: Israel finally arrives at the mountain where Moses first met God at the burning bush. God offers them a relationship: if they will obey Him, they will be His treasured people, a kingdom of priests, a holy nation. The people agree, but then God's actual appearing is so overwhelming, fire, smoke, thunder, a shaking mountain, that a strict boundary has to protect the people from death.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes a real historical meeting between God and Israel at a specific mountain. The covenant offered here is conditional and national, addressed to Israel as a people under the old covenant; the New Testament reapplies its language, treasured possession, kingdom of priests, holy nation, directly to the church, gathered not at a physical mountain but through Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: God calls Israel His segullah, a treasured possession, the personal property of a king, and promises they will be a mamlekhet kohanim, a kingdom of priests, mediating His presence to the world. Yet none of this comes without qadash, consecration, careful, deliberate preparation to meet a God whose holiness cannot be approached carelessly.
basic.typology: The terrifying, fenced-off mountain of Sinai, which none may touch on pain of death, stands in deliberate contrast to Mount Zion in the new covenant, which believers may approach with confidence through Christ, Hebrews 12:18-24.
basic.christ: Hebrews 12:18-24 explicitly contrasts this terrifying mountain with the access believers now have to God through Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant, whose blood speaks a better word than the blood demanded at Sinai's boundary. What Israel could only approach at mortal risk, believers now approach with confidence because of Christ.
basic.god: God is both intimate and terrifying in the same chapter: He calls Israel His treasured possession, carried on eagles' wings, and yet His holiness is so consuming that even priests must consecrate themselves or be broken out against. His love does not diminish His holiness; both are fully present at Sinai.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 12:18-24 contrasts Sinai's terror with the access believers have to Mount Zion through Christ. 1 Peter 2:9 applies treasured possession and kingdom of priests directly to the church. Titus 2:14 speaks of Christ purifying for Himself a people who are His own possession.
basic.oneStory: At Sinai, God offers covenant intimacy to a people He has just rescued, but reveals just how holy He is and how much preparation is needed to approach Him safely. The whole Bible moves from this fenced-off mountain toward the access believers now have through Christ, who makes it possible to draw near to the same holy God without fear.

context.literary: The chapter alternates between intimate covenant language, treasured possession, kingdom of priests, and escalating scenes of terrifying divine holiness, smoke, fire, trumpet blast, a shaking mountain, building deliberate tension before the giving of the Ten Commandments in the next chapter.
context.historical: Sinai becomes the founding covenant moment of Israel's national and religious identity, the mountain where the nation as a whole, not just Moses, meets God directly and receives both identity and law.
context.canonical: This scene establishes the pattern of covenant conditioned on obedience that runs through the rest of the Old Testament, while its language of treasured possession and priestly kingdom becomes directly reapplied to the church in the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish Israel's identity as a people in covenant with the LORD, chosen and treasured yet called to holiness, and to impress on them, through overwhelming sensory experience, the seriousness of approaching a holy God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical covenant-making event with Israel at a particular mountain; it does not prescribe that believers today must build physical boundaries or fear death for approaching God. Its enduring principle, God's holiness demands reverent preparation, and covenant blessing is tied to faithful obedience, carries forward, transformed by the access Christ now provides.

debates.question: Is the covenant offered in verses 5 to 6 conditional or unconditional?

views.label: Conditional on obedience
views.case: The text is explicit: 'if you will indeed obey My voice and keep My covenant,' making Israel's status as treasured possession and holy nation contingent on their faithfulness, consistent with the blessings and curses structure later spelled out in Deuteronomy 28.

views.label: Rooted in prior, unconditional grace
views.case: The offer comes only after God has already, unconditionally, carried Israel out of Egypt on eagles' wings, verse 4; the obedience called for is a grateful response to grace already given, not a bargain to earn rescue in the first place.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: Israel's redemption from Egypt was pure grace, but their ongoing covenant blessing as a nation was conditioned on faithful obedience, a pattern the New Testament transforms in Christ, whose obedience secures believers' standing apart from their own performance.

study.originalLanguages: The word segullah names Israel as God's treasured possession, the personal property of a king, set apart in value from every other nation. The phrase mamlekhet kohanim, kingdom of priests, envisions a whole nation mediating God's presence to the world, not merely a professional priestly class. And qadash, consecration, the washing of clothes and careful preparation, shows that meeting this God was never meant to be casual.

typology.type: Sinai, fenced off, terrifying, deadly to touch
typology.fulfillment: Contrasted directly in Hebrews 12:18-24 with Mount Zion, which believers approach freely through the blood of Christ.

typology.type: Israel as a kingdom of priests, a holy nation
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and expanded in the church, a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, 1 Peter 2:9.

study.christ: Hebrews 12 draws the sharpest possible contrast: Israel came to a mountain that could be touched, burning with fire, so terrifying that even Moses trembled, but believers have come to Mount Zion, to Jesus the mediator of a new covenant, whose sprinkled blood speaks a better word than the blood at Sinai's boundary. What required death to approach at Sinai now welcomes believers through Christ's finished work.
study.god: God is intimately personal, carrying Israel on eagles' wings and calling them His treasured possession, and utterly holy, so much so that even consecrated priests risk being broken out against if they approach carelessly. Sinai holds together, without contradiction, a God who saves in tenderness and a God who must be approached in reverent fear.

crossReferences.note: Contrasts the terror of Sinai with the confident access believers now have to God through Christ at Mount Zion.
Applies treasured possession, kingdom of priests, and holy nation directly to the church.
Christ gave Himself to purify for Himself a people who are His own possession, echoing segullah.
Moses later recalls this same fire, cloud, and thick darkness when the LORD spoke the covenant words.

study.oneStory: At Sinai, God offers a rescued people covenant intimacy while revealing just how holy He is, so holy that a mountain must be fenced and priests must be consecrated to approach Him safely. The whole story of the Bible moves from that fenced mountain toward Christ, who opens permanent, confident access to the same holy God for all who trust in Him.

sermon.title: The New City 2002
sermon.why: Preached on Exodus 19:2-8, 20:1-3, and 24:7-11, Keller explores Israel's covenant calling at Sinai to be a treasured possession and kingdom of priests, and how that calling is fulfilled and transformed in the church through Christ.

quiz.q: What does God remind Israel He has already done for them before offering the covenant in verses 4 to 6?

options.text: He carried them on eagles' wings and brought them to Himself.
options.note: Verse 4 gives this exact image, grace preceding the call to obedience.

options.text: He promised them wealth beyond what Egypt had.
options.note: The text emphasizes deliverance and relationship, not a promise of comparative wealth.

options.text: He gave them the land of Canaan outright with no further conditions.
options.note: The land promise exists elsewhere, but this passage centers on the covenant relationship being offered here.

options.text: Nothing; the covenant is presented with no prior grace mentioned.
options.note: Verse 4 explicitly recalls what God has already done before any condition is stated.

quiz.q: What three titles does God offer Israel if they obey His voice and keep His covenant?

options.text: His treasured possession, a kingdom of priests, and a holy nation.
options.note: Verses 5 to 6 give this exact threefold identity.

options.text: A great army, a wealthy nation, and a feared people.
options.note: The titles given are relational and priestly, not primarily military or economic.

options.text: A kingdom of prophets, a nation of scribes, and a treasured tribe.
options.note: The precise titles are treasured possession, kingdom of priests, and holy nation, not these terms.

options.text: No specific titles are offered.
options.note: Verses 5 to 6 give three specific titles explicitly.

quiz.q: How did the people respond to the terms of the covenant in verse 8?

options.text: They answered together, 'We will do everything that the LORD has spoken.'
options.note: This is their exact, unified response, a full commitment before the law was even given in detail.

options.text: They refused and asked for more time to decide.
options.note: The text records an immediate, unified acceptance, not hesitation.

options.text: Only Moses accepted; the people remained silent.
options.note: The text specifies all the people answered together.

options.text: They demanded to return to Egypt instead.
options.note: No such demand appears here; they commit to obedience.

quiz.q: What warning did God give about the boundary set around Mount Sinai?

options.text: Whoever touched the mountain must surely be put to death.
options.note: Verse 12 gives this severe warning, underscoring God's holiness.

options.text: Only women were forbidden from approaching the mountain.
options.note: The prohibition applied to all the people and even animals, not a single group.

options.text: There was no restriction on approaching the mountain.
options.note: A strict boundary with a death penalty for crossing it is explicitly established.

options.text: The boundary applied only to Moses.
options.note: Moses alone was permitted to ascend; the boundary specifically restricted everyone else, including priests without proper consecration.

quiz.q: How does Hebrews 12 use this scene at Sinai?

options.text: To contrast its terror with the confident access believers now have to God through Christ.
options.note: Hebrews 12:18-24 draws exactly this contrast between Sinai and Mount Zion in Christ.

options.text: To argue that Christians must observe the same physical boundary today.
options.note: Hebrews uses Sinai as a contrast to what believers now have in Christ, not as an ongoing physical requirement.

options.text: Hebrews never references this passage.
options.note: Hebrews 12:18-24 directly and explicitly references this scene at Sinai.

options.text: To argue Sinai was a myth with no historical basis.
options.note: Hebrews treats the Sinai event as a real, weighty historical reality precisely to make its contrast meaningful.

questions: God reminded Israel of His grace, eagles' wings, before ever stating a condition of obedience. How well do you keep grace and obedience in this same right order in your own walk with God?

Israel eagerly promised, 'we will do everything the LORD has spoken,' before they even knew the details of the law. Where have you made commitments to God that later proved harder to keep than you expected?

The mountain had to be fenced off because God's holiness was too much for an unprepared people to approach safely. How seriously do you take the holiness of God in your own approach to worship and prayer?
Hebrews says believers now approach not a mountain that burns with fire, but Mount Zion, through Jesus. Does your own sense of access to God feel more like Sinai's terror or Zion's welcome, and why?

Israel was called a kingdom of priests, meant to represent God to the world. Peter applies that same title to the whole church. How does it change your view of your ordinary week to know you are called to represent God in it?

translationNotes.note: Carried you on eagles' wings is a striking image combining strength and tenderness, an eagle both bearing its young to safety and teaching them to fly by supporting them in flight.
Treasured possession renders segullah, a term used elsewhere of royal personal property, distinct from the general wealth of a kingdom, underlining the intimacy of God's claim on Israel.

The priests mentioned here likely refers to those who already functioned in a priestly role prior to the formal establishment of the Aaronic priesthood later in Exodus, since the tribe of Levi had not yet been formally set apart.
